elements.sent-2411-001: XAI research often focuses on settings where people learn about and assess algorithmic systems individually.
elements.sent-2411-002: However, as more public AI systems are deployed, it becomes essential for XAI to facilitate collective understanding and deliberation.
elements.sent-2411-003: We conducted a task-based interview study involving 8 focus groups and 12 individual interviews to explore how explanations can support AI novices in understanding and forming opinions about AI systems.
elements.sent-2411-004: Participants received a collection of explanations organized into four information categories to solve tasks and decide about a system's deployment.
elements.sent-2411-005: These explanations improved or calibrated participants’ self-reported understanding and decision confidence and facilitated group discussions.
elements.sent-2411-006: Participants valued both technical and contextual information and the self-directed and modular explanation structure.
elements.sent-2411-007: Our contributions include an explanation approach that facilitates both individual and collaborative interaction and explanation design recommendations, including active and controllable exploration, different levels of information detail and breadth, and adaptations to the needs of decision subjects.
elements.sent-2411-008: A growing number of algorithmic decision-making systems (ADM systems) are deployed and used in the public sector to decide about critical issues, such as employment, migration, and criminal justice~\cite{scott_algorithmic_2022, Bansak2018, zuger_ai_2023, chouldechova2017 .}
elements.sent-2411-009: These systems can result in discrimination and damages for decision subjects ~\cite{brown_toward_2019, raji_fallacy_2022 , especially when decision-making is opaque and uncontestable~\cite{ananny_seeing_2018, alfrink2023, definelicht2020 .}}
elements.sent-2411-010: The field of explainable AI (XAI) aims to reduce these risks by developing methods to make ADM systems more understandable and facilitate their assessment and control~\cite{langer_what_2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-011: But while much of XAI research is focused on individuals, research has shown that learning about and understanding complex topics benefits from collaboration in group settings~\cite{smith_why_2009, moshman_collaborative_1998, nokes-malach_when_2015, navajas_aggregated_2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-012: Work on public AI further argues that multi-stakeholder deliberation is an essential step in ensuring public participation in deployment decisions of these systems~\cite{kawakami2024, soton2024, brown_toward_2019 , but how explanations can fulfill these demands is an open question in XAI.}
elements.sent-2411-013: Crucially, many people affected by decisions of ADM systems have no technical background in developing or analyzing these systems.
elements.sent-2411-014: That is, a majority of decision subjects, domain experts, and other stakeholders of the public are lay people~\cite{lima2023, devito2018, shulner-tal_enhancing_2022 or AI novices\footnote{We use the term in reference to "visualization novices"~\cite{burns2023 and synonymously with "lay people"~\cite{szymanski_visual_2021, shen_designing_2020, devito2018 .. .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-015: Explanations for AI novices naturally need to fulfill different requirements than for AI practitioners, as they have to be adapted to their background~\cite{ehsan_who_2024 , interests~\cite{langer_what_2021 , and prior knowledge~\cite{szymanski_visual_2021, cheng_explaining_2019, schmude2024information , which is why personalization~\cite{shulner-tal_enhancing_2022, conati_toward_2021, naiseh2020_personalising and interactivity~\cite{cheng_explaining_2019, bertrand2023, guesmi_interactive_2023 are central concepts in explanation design.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-016: Further, while explanation formats (e.g., interactive, visual, textual) are known to impact AI novices' understanding of algorithmic systems~\cite{cheng_explaining_2019, bove2022, szymanski_visual_2021 , the precise effect is inconsistent between studies~\cite{bove2022 due to contextual factors such as participants' perceptions of the use case domain.}}
elements.sent-2411-017: Previous qualitative research identified the information needs of AI novices~\cite{schmude2024information , emphasized user-preference for social and conversational formats~\cite{bertrand2023 , and employed participatory methods to design explanations in collaboration with end-users~\cite{weitz2024, lee_webuildai_2019 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-018: This work connects to these insights and draws from disciplines such as CSCW~\cite{convertino_cache_2008, fiesler_qualitative_2019 , cognitive science~\cite{keil2006, keil_folkscience_2003 , and educational psychology~\cite{smith_why_2009, nokes-malach_when_2015 to examine how explanations can support understanding and decision-making of AI novices in individual and collaborative settings~\cite{soton2024, weitz2024 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-019: Developing this design approach to improve understanding and facilitate deliberation meets multiple challenges.
elements.sent-2411-020: Group composition and dynamics place special demands on explanation design~\cite{naiseh_explainable_2021 , as explanations must cover a diverse set of information needs~\cite{schmude2024information, bove_why_2024 and format preferences~\cite{bertrand2023 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-021: In addition, they must support collaborative interaction~\cite{long_role_2021 , such as sharing and simultaneous processing of information.}
elements.sent-2411-022: That is, explanations need to provide comprehensive information but remain clear and navigable, and should foster group interaction but still be suitable for individual settings.
elements.sent-2411-023: We address these challenges by proposing a modular explanation design that spans four information categories from which users can select information according to their needs.
elements.sent-2411-024: Additionally, when validating explanation approaches qualitatively, it is crucial to contact the relevant stakeholder groups, which can be especially challenging with decision subjects in minority groups or difficult situations, who arguably have the highest risk of incurring negative consequences from ADM.
elements.sent-2411-025: To address this, we conducted two focus groups with decision subjects to include their perspectives and voices in developing explanations for AI systems in the public sector~\cite{brown_toward_2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-026: This paper explores how explanations can be used in individual and collaborative settings to support AI novices' learning, interaction, and decision-making regarding the deployment of ADM systems.
elements.sent-2411-027: We present the findings of a task-based interview study with 43 AI novices involving 8 focus groups and 12 single interviews.
elements.sent-2411-028: We propose an explanation design that addresses AI novices' information needs and applies current best practices in XAI~\cite{byrne_good_2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-029: The design consists of a collection of 36 explanations in the form of question-answer pairs organized into the four categories data, system details, usage, and context, which are further subdivided into subtopics and levels of detail (\autoref{fig:explanation_overview ).}
elements.sent-2411-030: Participants used these explanations to solve the study tasks and decide about deploying an ADM system (\autoref{fig:study_procedure ).}
elements.sent-2411-031: The study's use case is an algorithm for the prediction of job-seekers' employability, connecting to previous work on ADM systems in employment~\cite{scott_algorithmic_2022, niklas2015, lopez_reinforcing_2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-032: We analyze participants' self-reported understanding, decision confidence, and perceptions of key information, and conduct a thematic analysis of group interactions with explanations.
elements.sent-2411-033: We discuss explanation design suggestions and the benefits and challenges of using explanations in collaborative settings.
elements.sent-2411-034: In the analysis of our findings, we put particular focus on the following research questions:.
elements.sent-2411-035: Explanations: How does a question-driven, modular explanation design support AI novices' understanding in individual and group settings?.
elements.sent-2411-036: Deliberation: How do the explanations support group interaction with information and deliberation processes?.
elements.sent-2411-037: The contributions of our work include i) the validation of an explanation design building on current XAI principles, including question-driven and modular design, different levels of completeness and soundness, and suitability for both individual and collective settings; ii) an analysis of the kind and amount of information that participants requested most often and perceived as most important; iii) an analysis of how our explanations support group collaboration in solving tasks and group deliberation in forming opinions about ADM systems; and iv) recommendations for the design and use of these explanations and implications for the incorporation of group settings in XAI.
elements.sent-2411-038: In this section, we briefly describe how our work connects to human-centered AI, research on explanation design for AI novices, and literature on understanding, interaction, and decision-making in groups.
elements.sent-2411-039: Explainability is often described as a cornerstone of responsible AI systems\footnote{While not every ADM system includes AI, we use both terms as we focus on decision-making systems with machine learning components.}
elements.sent-2411-040: Similar concepts are embedded in the notion of human-centered AI~\cite{capel_what_2023 , which proposes to 1) build systems based on user observation and stakeholder engagement and 2) empower rather than replace people by making these systems controllable and autonomy-preserving~\cite{ShneidermanBen2022HA, shin_algorithms_2023, xu2019 .}}
elements.sent-2411-041: These measures become especially important in high-risk settings~\cite{european_commission_laying_2021 , such as employment~\cite{scott_algorithmic_2022, flugge_perspectives_2021 , immigration~\cite{Bansak2018 , or criminal justice~\cite{chouldechova2017 , where erroneous and non-transparent algorithmic decisions can cause severe harm to those affected~\cite{raji_fallacy_2022 .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-042: To better understand how explanations can contribute to these principles, human-centered explainable AI places emphasis on the sociotechnical dimensions of explaining AI systems~\cite{ehsan_human-centered_2023 in that it assumes that transparency alone is not enough to make AI systems explainable~\cite{ananny_seeing_2018 , but that explanations need to consider the system's social context~\cite{wenzelburger_algorithms_2022 , its lifecycle~\cite{dhanorkar_who_2021 and its different stakeholder groups~\cite{ehsan_human-centered_2023 .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-043: In the context of this work, human-centered explainability is realized by testing and validating a design approach that has been developed based on qualitative exploration of AI novices' information needs~\cite{schmude2024information .}
elements.sent-2411-044: Further, our work aims to examine suitable formats for explanations that can accompany the deployment of AI systems in public institutions~\cite{definelicht2020 and inform approaches of practicing responsible AI in high-risk application settings~\cite{ehsan_human-centered_2023 .}}
elements.sent-2411-045: Explanations for non-technical audiences have different requirements than those for AI practitioners.
elements.sent-2411-046: Explanation methods like LIME~\cite{Ribeiro2016 , SHAP~\cite{lundberg2017 , and surrogate models~\cite{molnar2022 are better suited to experts due to their reliance on previous knowledge and technical literacy, while effective methods for AI novices are still being developed.}}}
elements.sent-2411-047: As transparency does not equal understanding~\cite{ananny_seeing_2018 , simply making all information about a system available is no valid explanation strategy.}
elements.sent-2411-048: Instead, explanations must be selected and designed to be suitable to the recipient's stakeholder role~\cite{langer_what_2021 , prior knowledge~\cite{schmude2024information , beliefs~\cite{miller_explanation_2019 , and explanatory stance~\cite{byrne_good_2023, keil2006 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-049: Various studies examined how such explanations for AI novices might be realized: \citet{cheng_explaining_2019 showed that "white-box" explanations (transparent models) can improve "objective"\footnote{We use the term in reference to~\citet{cheng_explaining_2019 and~\citet{bove2022 , it means to describe factual or testable understanding.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-050: However,~\citet{bove2022 used similar explanations and found that they had the opposite effect on participant understanding, which they attribute to a difference in the studies' use case domains (student admission vs. finance).}
elements.sent-2411-051: Other studies underscore this variability, indicating that comprehension varies with personal and demographic factors and domain knowledge~\cite{wang21, shulner-tal_enhancing_2022, ehsan_who_2024 and that personalized explanations could be effective in addressing this~\cite{conati_toward_2021, shulner-tal_enhancing_2022 .}}
elements.sent-2411-052: Further aspects to be considered include the explanation's purpose~\cite{freiesleben2023 and the recipient's familiarity with AI~\cite{kramer2018 .}}
elements.sent-2411-053: Regarding the information needs, previous qualitative research outlined that AI novices value information about the context and intention of a system's deployment~\cite{schmude2024information, kawakami2024 as well about the responsible institution~\cite{brown_toward_2019 .}}
elements.sent-2411-054: Notably,~\citet{lee_webuildai_2019 and~\citet{weitz2024 conducted participatory workshops to design explanations with end-users in the public sector, finding that participants valued the collaborative settings and their improved understanding of algorithmic systems.}}
elements.sent-2411-055: Drawing from these approaches, this work focuses on the design and qualitative validation of explanations such that they address the information needs of AI novices~\cite{schmude2024information and support their understanding~\cite{langer_what_2021 and decision-making~\cite{bertrand2023 in individual and collective settings.}}}
elements.sent-2411-056: Our explanation design draws from various approaches in previous work.
elements.sent-2411-057: The information base used for the explanations was compiled from different sources documenting the development and planned deployment of employment prediction algorithms~\cite{scott_algorithmic_2022, allhutter_bericht_ams-algorithmus_2020 , producing an extensive collection of "scavenged"~\cite{wieringa_hey_2023 material.}}
elements.sent-2411-058: To structure this collection, we drew from~\citet{lim+dey_assessing_intelligibility2009 's work on intelligibility types, ~\citet{liao2020 's concept of question-driven explanation design, and~\citet{schmude2024information 's separation of information categories into data, system details, usage, and context.}}}
elements.sent-2411-059: We further apply~\citet{kulesza_too_2013 's principles on explanation soundness (fidelity, complexity) and completeness (coverage, density) and draw from works that apply these principles in explanation design~\cite{chatti_is_2022, guesmi_interactive_2023, kulesza_principles_2015 , by introducing a structure of sub-topics and a hierarchy of explanation levels.}}

elements.subsection-2411-003: Understanding and decision-making in collaborative settings
elements.sent-2411-061: Improving understanding is a central goal of explanations~\cite{langer_what_2021 and enables people to pursue their goals, such as assessing or contesting a system's decisions~\cite{hoffman_explainable_2023 .}}
elements.sent-2411-062: However, understanding can be defined in numerous ways~\cite{grimm_varieties_2019, baumberger_what_2017, zagzebski_toward_2019, keil2006 .}
elements.sent-2411-063: This work draws from literature in the learning sciences, cognitive sciences, and XAI to define understanding as i) connecting and applying information, whereas "knowledge" only stores information~\cite{grimm_varieties_2019, baumberger_what_2017 , ii) being the attempt to grasp the underlying structure of a phenomenon by way of simplification~\cite{zagzebski_toward_2019 , iii) consisting of several "facets" that include both the analytical and the emotional connection to information~\cite{wiggins_understanding_2005 , and iv) rarely being exhaustive, but rather a "working" mental model that is attained by recognizing and filling gaps until the learner deems it sufficient~\cite{keil2006 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-064: Due to the challenge of defining and measuring understanding~\cite{sato_testing_2019 , recent research has proposed an "abilities-based" approach~\cite{speith_conceptualizing_2024 which defines understanding through the skills the learner possesses, connecting to comparable operationalizations by the learning sciences~\cite{wiggins_understanding_2005 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-065: While individual understanding has been the subject of many studies in XAI~\cite{chen2023_machine_explanations, cheng_explaining_2019, wang21, schmude2023 , understanding in group settings has been less explored.}
elements.sent-2411-066: However, different disciplines have investigated collaborative understanding in detail: In the cognitive sciences, distributed cognition~\cite{keil_folkscience_2003 and outsourced understanding~\cite{keil2006 are components for the analysis of explanatory understanding, whereas educational psychology produced insight on the effects of group tutoring~\cite{nokes-malach_when_2015, smith_why_2009, moshman_collaborative_1998, navajas_aggregated_2018 and comparisons to one-on-one tutoring~\cite{bloom_2_1984 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-067: Further, research in the field of computer-supported collaborative work provides approaches for the analysis of collaborative settings~\cite{fiesler_qualitative_2019 , group composition~\cite{convertino_cache_2008 , and group interactions~\cite{sutcliffe_applying_2005 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-068: Recent research on public AI~\cite{zuger_ai_2023 has emphasized the importance of including stakeholders by introducing deliberation early in the design process~\cite{kawakami2024, yurrita2022, zhang_deliberating_2023 .}}
elements.sent-2411-069: Further, recent work in XAI has begun to consider how explanations for group interactions could be approached, describing that "many-to-one" interactions (multiple people interacting with an explanation) will likely differ enormously from "one-to-one" interactions due to "complexities in group dynamics, cognitive bias amplification, trust issues within the group, and group-centric evaluation"~\cite{soton2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-070: Drawing from these conceptualizations, this work aims to explore empirically how explanations can support group deliberation by improving understanding and informing decision-making processes.
elements.sent-2411-071: In this section, we describe our methods and study procedure.
elements.sent-2411-072: We conducted a task-based semi-structured interview study with 43 participants recruited from civil society organizations, a job agency, and the author's extended network (Section~\ref{sec:participants ), structured into 8 focus groups (3--5 participants) and 12 single interviews.}
elements.sent-2411-073: Participants were presented with the study's employment prediction use case (Section~\ref{sec:use_case ) and received a collection of explanations about this system (Section~\ref{sec:explanation_design ) before solving four tasks and making decisions about the system's deployment (Section~\ref{sec:study_procedure ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-074: The study closed with an interview, lasting 90--120 minutes for focus groups and 60 minutes for single interviews.
elements.sent-2411-075: We analyzed the explanations' effect, participants' prioritization of information, and the individual and collective interaction with the explanations (Section~\ref{sec:analysis ).}
elements.sent-2411-076: The university’s research ethics committee approved this study.
elements.subsection-2411-004: Use case: The employment prediction algorithm
elements.sent-2411-077: The employment prediction algorithm\footnote{More detailed information on the use case has been omitted to adhere to the anonymization policy but will be re-inserted for the final version.}
elements.sent-2411-078: It uses a logistic regression model trained on historical data to assess how demographic features (e.g., age, education, nationality) influence employment chances, producing short-term and long-term employment scores as recommendations.
elements.sent-2411-079: Counselors use these recommendations to decide about adequate support measures for the job-seeker (courses, stabilizing measures, etc.) but can override the system's recommendations with justification.
elements.sent-2411-080: The system is comparable to algorithmic profiling tools that are used in various countries to assist in job-seeker assessment and resource allocation~\cite{scott_algorithmic_2022, lopez_reinforcing_2019, niklas2015 and which repeatedly highlight the limits of automation in interpersonal interaction~\cite{agentur_für_arbeit_2021, niklas2015, DESIERE_STRUYVEN_2021, scott_algorithmic_2022 .}}
elements.sent-2411-081: Due to its documentation and exemplification of typical ADM systems' challenges in public employment, the employment prediction algorithm was thus chosen as the use case for this study.
elements.sent-2411-082: Our explanation design was composed of 36 question-answer pairs about the employment prediction algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-083: Each question belonged to one of four categories, data (format, content, limitations), system details (features, model, examples), usage (operation by and interaction with users), and context (intention of deployment, target group, responsible actors), which were informed by work about the information needs of AI novices~\cite{schmude2024information .}
elements.sent-2411-084: Each category was further divided into subtopics with three levels of increasing detail (base level, level 2, level 3).
elements.sent-2411-085: Every explanation contained a question (e.g., "Who operates the system?") answered with a brief text or image printed on an A5 paper sheet (\autoref{fig:explanation_examples ).}
elements.sent-2411-086: Participants first received an explanation overview (\autoref{fig:explanation_overview ) and the four base explanations and could request levels 2 and 3 at any time during the explanation phase (as depicted in~\autoref{fig:study_procedure ).}}
elements.sent-2411-087: The structure of the explanations was intended to i) facilitate uptake by providing information in a modular format and ii) provide different levels of both soundness (information levels) and completeness (subtopics) of information~\cite{kulesza_too_2013, kulesza_principles_2015, chatti_is_2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-088: The selection of subtopics and information levels was guided by work on ADM systems in public employment~\cite{scott_algorithmic_2022, niklas2015, allhutter_bericht_ams-algorithmus_2020 and research on the prioritization of information by AI novices~\cite{schmude2024information, weitz2024 .}}
elements.sent-2411-089: The self-directed provision of information allowed participants to select explanations according to their interests~\cite{conati_toward_2021 and information needs.}
elements.sent-2411-090: The question-answer style was motivated by work on question-driven explanations~\cite{liao2020 and was intended to encourage engagement.}
elements.sent-2411-091: The explanations made use of different techniques that include feature importance, local and global explanations, examples, counterfactuals, and argumentative approaches (\autoref{fig:explanation_examples ).}
elements.sent-2411-092: Lastly, the explanations were presented in an analog paper format to encourage interactions such as handing around paper sheets, pointing, and reading aloud.
elements.figure-2411-001: [H] \includegraphics[width=\textwidth, bb=0 0 1200 442]{images/Explanation_overview.pdf} \caption[Explanation overview]{Overview of explanations. Explanations were designed as a collection of 36 question-answer pairs. The questions were assigned to 4 categories -- data, system details, usage, and context -- each containing 9 questions. Participants received the base explanations at the beginning of the explanation phase, as indicated by the ticked boxes, and could request all other explanations at any time during the explanation phase using this overview.} \label{fig:explanation_overview}
elements.figure-2411-002: [H] \includegraphics[width=380pt, bb=0 0 1226 865]{images/Explanation_examples.pdf} \caption[Explanation examples]{Four explanation examples. Examples for explanations in the categories data, system details, usage, and context. Each single question was printed on a sheet of A5 paper together with a short answer to the question. elements.sent-2411-093: The study procedure consisted of several steps that are depicted in \autoref{fig:study_procedure .}
elements.sent-2411-094: This section describes the introduction of the use case, the explanation phase comprising exploration, tasks, and the group decision, and the mechanisms for participants' self-reports on understanding, confidence, and other aspects.
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth, bb=0 0 1799 546]{images/Study_procedure.pdf} \caption[Study procedure]{Overview of the study procedure. Participants filled out consent forms and demographic questionnaires and received a (mock) newspaper article with key information about the ADM system. At this point, participants had to give a first report of their initial understanding, deployment decision, and decision confidence. They then received the explanations, completed four tasks, and groups decided collectively about the system's deployment. Participants then again reported their understanding, individual decision, and decision confidence. Further, they reported the most helpful and influential explanation categories and their perceived inclusion and voice in the group discussion. Interview questions were asked during the second individual report. Finally, participants were invited to share additional comments, were compensated, and thanked for their time. The process for single interviews differed from focus groups in that the collective decision and questions about inclusion and voice in the group were omitted.} \label{fig:study_procedure}
elements.sent-2411-096: Participants received initial information about the employment prediction algorithm in the form of a mock newspaper article.
elements.sent-2411-097: The article provided key information and featured the perspectives of employers and employee associations.. Exploration phase.
elements.sent-2411-098: After receiving the explanation overview and base level explanations of each category, participants had 15 minutes (focus groups) or 20 minutes (interviews) to become familiar with the structure and mark relevant explanations.
elements.sent-2411-099: Participants could freely decide which explanations to read and request and were encouraged to discuss them.. Tasks.
elements.sent-2411-100: Participants then received a fictional job-seeker case example describing Mr. Harald G.\footnote{Details on the inspiration for this case example where omitted to adhere to anonymization policy, but will be re-inserted for the final version.}
elements.sent-2411-101: Participants solved four tasks formulated as questions covering i) the job-seeker's ability to change information stored about him (such as education, physical impairment, etc.), ii) his employability scoring by the employment prediction algorithm, iii) the support measures he would receive, and iv) his ability to contest the decision.
elements.sent-2411-102: All questions had multiple answers with one correct choice and could be answered with information present in the explanations in different categories and detail levels.
elements.sent-2411-103: Whereas tasks 1, 3, and 4 required mostly information retrieval, task 2 could be solved in two ways: by either giving an estimate based on the rough weightings in the system details base explanations or by calculating the precise employment score.
elements.sent-2411-104: Participants had 15 (focus groups) or 20 (single interviews) minutes to solve the tasks.
elements.sent-2411-105: During that time, they could access and request all explanations and discuss possible solutions.. Decision task.
elements.sent-2411-106: Lastly, participants in focus groups had 10 minutes to discuss the system's deployment and make a collective yes / no decision.
elements.sent-2411-107: If no consensual decision was reached in time, participants either decided themselves how to resolve the situation (e.g., majority vote) or received the option of stating conditions for the system's deployment.. Self-reports.
elements.sent-2411-108: Participants reported their understanding, decision confidence, and inclusion and voice in the group with 5-point Likert scales, their decision on the system's deployment with yes / no voting ballots, and their choice of the explanation categories most "helpful for their understanding" and "influential to their decisions" by selecting the categories that applied.
elements.sent-2411-109: Self-reports were conducted using a color-coded system to ensure anonymity during focus groups.
elements.sent-2411-110: Each participant was assigned a color and given matching reporting material.
elements.sent-2411-111: During the second self-report, the investigator also asked interview questions about participants' interaction with the explanations, their understanding processes, the most relevant information, and any additional situational questions.
elements.sent-2411-112: In single interviews, the collective decision and questions about inclusion and voice in the group were omitted.
elements.sent-2411-113: All focus groups and interviews were audio-recorded and transcribed.
elements.sent-2411-114: These transcripts, participants' self-reports during the studies, and the investigators' field notes, provided the data basis for analysis.
elements.sent-2411-115: For RQ1-Explanations, we focus on how the explanations helped and impacted participants' understanding of the ADM system and examine the differences between individual and group settings.
elements.sent-2411-116: For this, we compare participants' self-reported understanding before and after the explanation phases, their performance in the study tasks, and which and how many explanations were requested.
elements.sent-2411-117: Further, we conduct an inductive thematic analysis~\cite{braun_using_2006 of participants' responses to the investigator's interview questions, capturing articulations about the strengths and weaknesses of the explanations' design, the impact of the time limit, understanding barriers, and information perceived as most relevant.}
elements.sent-2411-118: While the quantitative items thus serve to characterize the diversity of participants' perceptions~\cite{maxwell2010 and facilitate qualitative exploration~\cite{Weiss1995 , they are not intended to invoke "inference [...] of greater generality"~\cite{maxwell2010 nor impose a mental model based on variance theory~\cite{patton1990 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-119: For RQ2-Deliberation, we first focus on how participants shared and combined information to solve the study tasks and, second, on how explanations enabled group deliberation.
elements.sent-2411-120: For the first part, we conduct an inductive and deductive thematic analysis of the groups' interaction processes with explanations, connecting our descriptions of these interaction processes to mechanisms for collaborative success and failures as described by~\citet{nokes-malach_when_2015 .}
elements.sent-2411-121: For the second part, we compare self-reported decision confidence before and after participants received and discussed the explanations, summarize their feedback about the group setting, and conduct an inductive thematic analysis of focus group conversations in which explanations drove group deliberation and convergence, facilitating the opinion-forming of participants.
elements.sent-2411-122: Participants were recruited through cooperation with civil society organizations, an employment agency, and the authors' extended network.
elements.sent-2411-123: Recruitment criteria included full legal age and no technical knowledge about AI systems, i.e., AI novices.
elements.sent-2411-124: All studies were conducted in person in office or public spaces.
elements.sent-2411-125: Participants were compensated with 30€ for participation in focus groups (90--120 minutes) and 20€ for single interviews (60 minutes).
elements.sent-2411-126: Our approach for organizing, composing, and moderating focus groups was informed by~\citet{KruegerRichardA2004Fg:a .}
elements.sent-2411-127: Concerning the participant sample size, we are guided by research on qualitative methods, which suggests that the number of participants should be determined by code and meaning saturation~\cite{hennink_code_2017 .. Choice of participants.}
elements.sent-2411-128: Participants were selected to be representatives of one of three roles: domain experts, decision subjects, or members of the general public.
elements.sent-2411-129: We define domain experts as people who are competent in the field that the ADM system is used in, such as job counselors or advisors (groups 2, 3, 7, and 8).

elements.sent-2411-137: Participants reported their understanding before and after receiving and discussing the explanations.
elements.sent-2411-138: Participants gave divergent feedback on how they perceived the explanations, describing their structure and design as "nicely presented" (A2, C2), "good to get an overview" (C3, H4), "well-selected" regarding the questions (G2), "active and controllable" (S8), and as a good primer (F4).
elements.sent-2411-139: Critical comments described them as "too much" (D1, S4), "at first overwhelming" (H5), "confusing" (B1, D1), "theoretical" (D2), and "quite complex" (C2).
elements.sent-2411-140: However, this was not always seen as an issue: "I believe that it is complex.
elements.sent-2411-141: But I also believe that it needs to be." (D2).
elements.sent-2411-142: Participants gave nuanced reasons why their perceived understanding decreased.
elements.sent-2411-143: Several focus group participants stated they did not fully understand the technical explanations, including numbers and a formula (B3, C3, G4).
elements.sent-2411-144: In contrast, participants in single interviews with comparable backgrounds met fewer challenges with technical explanations, often stating that they appreciated applying this knowledge when solving the tasks (S2, S8, S11).
elements.sent-2411-145: In these cases, the setting likely mediated the ease of interaction, as participants in single interviews could concentrate without distraction (further described in Section~\ref{sec:comparison_single_vs_group +~\ref{section:discussion_groups ).}}
elements.sent-2411-146: However, irrespective of the setting, confrontation with mathematical details seemed to re-adjust participants' benchmarks for "high understanding" and calibrate their perceptions.
elements.sent-2411-147: Likewise, several participants stated that the different subtopics and levels of detail in the explanations helped them identify the information they were still missing to judge the whole system (B1, F3, G3).
elements.sent-2411-148: Both observations indicate that the explanations revealed participants' blind spots and helped them to overcome the "illusion of explanatory depth"~\cite{rozenblit2002 -- an overestimation of one's understanding due to limited knowledge and misled intuition -- which is exemplified by S2's comment: "The more I look at the explanations now, the more I realize that I simply assumed a lot of things but didn't actually know how it worked." In short, participants gauged their understanding relative to the amount of information available.}
elements.sent-2411-149: That is, since participants only had time to read through parts of all available explanations and were aware that they had not seen all of them, they reported a decrease in perceived understanding when it, in fact, improved.
elements.sent-2411-150: The implications for measuring explanations' effect on understanding are discussed in Section~\ref{sec:discussion .}
elements.subsubsection-2411-002: The explanation design was not equally suited to every participant group.
elements.sent-2411-151: In contrast to participants whose perceived understanding was improved and calibrated by the explanations, we observed that the explanations did not fully support understanding or interaction for some groups.
elements.sent-2411-152: In these cases, the explanations' language and textual format were listed as impeding factors.
elements.sent-2411-153: The language style was described discrepantly as "well understandable" (P3, E3, H1) but also "very difficult" (E2, F2) and "too technical" (B3, B4) depending on participants' background.
elements.sent-2411-154: Crucially, in Groups E and F (decision subjects), participants stated that the language posed challenges.
elements.sent-2411-155: E2 compared the explanations to official letters they receive: "I get letters that I don't understand from the court.
elements.sent-2411-156: I understand every single word, but I don't understand the context.” In line with this, job counselors in Groups C, G, and H stated that explanations in simple language would be required if they were supposed to be useful for their job-seeking clients.
elements.sent-2411-157: Similar comments were made about the explanations' heavy reliance on textual information, described as demanding and requiring plenty of concentration.
elements.sent-2411-158: While previous work found that textual explanations can be effective despite users' dispreference~\cite{szymanski_visual_2021 or are even preferred~\cite{weitz2024 , a more interactive and visual design would likely facilitate interaction: "If there's a program where, when you click on it, it opens as a window and you can read about it, that would be great, and you can deal with it at your own pace." (H5) Both, simpler language options and an interactive version, are thus useful suggestions for improving the explanation design to ensure that explanations are helpful for a broader range of stakeholder groups.}}
elements.sent-2411-159: Further, Section~\ref{sec:group_interaction_with_info describes how these understanding challenges impacted group interaction.}
elements.subsubsection-2411-003: Time limits led to forced prioritization of information, with different effects on participants.
elements.sent-2411-160: All participants had 40 minutes to become familiar with the explanation structure, request new explanations, solve the given tasks, and, in focus groups, make a collective decision (\autoref{fig:decision_table ).}
elements.sent-2411-161: The time limit was meant to incentivize participants to prioritize information and, where possible, work together to process larger amounts.
elements.sent-2411-162: This succeeded for most focus group's participants, who stated that "when you have little time, the teamwork is extremely good" (B2) and that "even a brief exchange is enough, otherwise it becomes too much" (C3).
elements.sent-2411-163: However, the time limit also led to induced stress, especially in single interviews (S2, S6, S7, S8, S9, S11).
elements.sent-2411-164: S9 commented that this reminded them of their work as a social center manager: "Basically, the time to read about something new does not exist." (S9).
elements.sent-2411-165: This points to the need for brief and compact synopses complementing the detailed explanations.
elements.sent-2411-166: Regarding prioritization, many participants liked choosing explanations according to their own interest, which gave the feeling of being in control (S8), provided a direction for exploration (H1), and justified skipping explanation categories in favor of others (S6, S9).
elements.sent-2411-167: Allowing for the self-directed selection of relevant information by users could thus be beneficial to encourage interaction and engagement.
elements.subsubsection-2411-004: Some single participants worked through surprising amounts of explanations.
elements.sent-2411-168: Compared to focus groups, participants in single interviews often requested and engaged with equal or even higher amounts of explanations (as depicted in \autoref{fig:explanations_per_study ).}
elements.sent-2411-169: Often, participants in single interviews requested "the whole context" (S3) or "all of level 3" (S4) of one category, appearing to deal with the amount of information easily.
elements.sent-2411-170: A likely reason for this is that single interviews supported more focused and in-depth interaction with explanations compared to group settings.
elements.sent-2411-171: Participants in single interviews also performed better in the study tasks overall and, as mentioned above, often calculated the job-seeker's precise employment chance in task 2, which required following a multi-step process.
elements.sent-2411-172: This is noteworthy, as none of the groups, aside from the pilot groups, attempted to calculate the precise chance.
elements.sent-2411-173: This cannot be explained by a difference in educational or professional background, as even focus groups with university graduates in domain expert professions did not perform these calculations, again indicating that the reason might be the difference in supported interaction.
elements.sent-2411-174: For example, the parallel processing of information might have impeded understanding of the system's entirety in groups, as described by G3: "You only pick out the most important things.
elements.sent-2411-175: And then you haven't gathered information about the thing as a whole." This highlights the benefits and trade-offs of both settings, suggesting that individual engagement with explanations might be helpful before discussing them in a group setting.
elements.sent-2411-176: The implications of this are discussed further in Section~\ref{sec:discussion.}

elements.subsubsection-2411-005: All explanation categories were deemed helpful and influential, but system details
elements.sent-2411-177: In the second self-reports, participants were asked which explanation category was most helpful for their understanding, most influential for their decisions, and whether specific explanations stood out.
elements.sent-2411-178: Regarding understanding, participants in focus groups responded with a rather even distribution over all categories, as depicted in~\autoref{fig:explanations_most_helpful , and frequently mentioned that "all of them [are relevant]...}
elements.sent-2411-179: I don't think you can leave anything out, really" (D3).
elements.sent-2411-180: Both participants in focus groups and single interviews further stated that system details were crucial, as they were perceived as "tangible" (S6), "concrete" (S8), and to be "the devil in the detail" (B4).
elements.sent-2411-181: Specifically, participants found explanations about the features and weighting most important (D1, G1, H3, S5), which G4 commented with: "That is the central point, the basis of the whole system." Even though context was less often reported as an influential explanation category, participants requested these explanations most often (\autoref{fig:explanations_overall ) and frequently mentioned affected persons' inability to contest decisions (C1, D1, E1) and the system's political background (A1, S3) as relevant -- both belonging to context.}
elements.sent-2411-182: Notably, participants in single interviews found data much less helpful and less influential, stating, e.g., that GDPR would likely cover the important aspects of it (S8) or that they prioritized another category in the time available: "I didn't deal with it in such detail [...].
elements.sent-2411-183: You read it [the base level] and already feel informed.
elements.sent-2411-184: As is often the case with data protection, 'yeah, close enough'." (S6).
elements.sent-2411-185: Other participants stated that the technical details were not too important for them but that they instead focused on how the system was used practically and which values it represented (S3, S10), corresponding to the categories usage and context.

elements.subsection-2411-010: RQ2-Deliberation: How do the explanations support group interaction with information and deliberation processes?
elements.sent-2411-186: In this section, we describe how our explanations supported group collaboration in exchanging information (Section \ref{sec:group_interaction_with_info ) and in discussing and forming opinions about the system's deployment (Section \ref{sec:pivotal_topics_in_groups ).}}
elements.subsubsection-2411-006: Explanations support collaborative success if group dynamic is constructive.
elements.sent-2411-187: We identify several processes that groups used to interact with information collectively.
elements.sent-2411-188: Our descriptions of these interaction processes include splitting (by category, order, priority), sharing (swapping, reading out loud, proposing solutions), analyzing (discussing, explaining), and combining (merging, locating, correcting).
elements.sent-2411-189: The degree to which groups used these processes appeared to depend on the familiarity between group members, their domain expertise, and their understanding.
elements.sent-2411-190: In the following, we analyze three exemplary conversation excerpts that showcase different forms of interaction in focus groups while also demonstrating the diversity in collective interaction.
elements.sent-2411-191: For each excerpt, we describe the interaction processes that emerged and then connect them to mechanisms that drive collaborative failure or success (marked by quotation marks) as outlined by~\citet{nokes-malach_when_2015 .}
elements.sent-2411-192: Case 1: Information sharing leads to increased engagement and construction of common ground.
elements.sent-2411-193: Group G was composed of participants who hold executive positions in a civil society organization and are responsible for the management and training of employees.
elements.sent-2411-194: Participants in this group showed high engagement with information, frequently sharing explanations and exchanging their perspectives by tapping on their professional experience.
elements.sent-2411-195: The excerpt begins several minutes after participants received the explanations just as G4 examines an explanation comparing the algorithmic score for two job-seekers:.
elements.sent-2411-196: G4: That's bad, the two of them.
elements.sent-2411-197: Look, I've got this [system details-C-base], "What chances would the system calculate for Martin and Schifteh?" (sharing: reading out loud) G2: Schifteh is probably worse off, isn't she? (analyzing: discussing) G4: Schifteh has 30\% chance of employment and Martin 52\%, even though Schifteh has a degree and would be working in the IT sector.
elements.sent-2411-198: And Martin has compulsory schooling and works in the cleaning sector.
elements.sent-2411-199: Martin's chances of employment are almost twice as high as Schifteh's.
elements.sent-2411-200: People who live in [this district], or in a district that is not well regarded, have totally negative...
elements.sent-2411-201: Framed in the context of mechanisms that drive collaborative success, G4 first draws the group's attention to an explanation they deem relevant, describing the algorithm's treatment of two different cases of job-seekers.
elements.sent-2411-202: G4 reads the information to the group, which prompts G2 and G3 to contribute their own perspectives, "increasing engagement" while also "cueing their prior knowledge".
elements.sent-2411-203: As a result, G1 is also engaged by the information and articulates interest in looking at it.
elements.sent-2411-204: In combination, the interaction has established "common ground" by sharing information the group can use for further collaboration.
elements.sent-2411-205: Interactions like these were common in Groups G and H (job counselors and trainers), indicating that the explanations' format and content suited these compositions.
elements.sent-2411-206: Case 2: Discussion of solutions leads to correction.
elements.sent-2411-207: Group B consisted of both staff members and volunteers of a civil society organization.
elements.sent-2411-208: The group interaction was mostly driven by one participant who requested and shared most of the information and thus acted as a guide and informer, potentially driven by her role as deputy manager.
elements.sent-2411-209: While this led to a more one-sided dynamic, the other participants later appreciated outsourcing part of the understanding process to B3.
elements.sent-2411-210: In the excerpt below, B3 proposes a solution for task 2 (chance of employment that the system assigns Harald G.; group low, medium, or high) that is then discussed:.
elements.sent-2411-211: B3: I chose “group low” for task 2.
elements.sent-2411-212: And the knee problems could be a “health impairment”.
elements.sent-2411-213: And I think the fact that he hasn't been employed much in the last 4 years has a very negative effect.
elements.sent-2411-214: What information do you have that speaks against that? (combining: correcting; analyzing: discussing) B1: Because with 50, he would still be in "group medium".
elements.sent-2411-215: That doesn't make up for anything.
elements.sent-2411-216: The group members in this situation "negotiate multiple perspectives" on the correct solution, notice discrepancies in their "common ground," and "re-expose" themselves to information to "correct errors" in their knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-217: B3 led the interaction and corrected other participants repeatedly, which could have caused adverse reactions from them (e.g., due to being "evaluated").
elements.sent-2411-218: However, B2 and B4 in fact later articulated appreciation for this interaction, stating that "B3 filtered that out well" (B2) and that "it was helpful that you [B3] always read it out and corrected it straight away" (B4).
elements.sent-2411-219: Correction was therefore seen as constructive to the discussion, possibly because of B3's leading role in the organization and because participants already knew each other.
elements.sent-2411-220: Case 3: Attempted error-correction fails to create common ground.
elements.sent-2411-221: Group E was composed of people who were job-seeking at the time of the interview or in the past.

elements.sent-2411-349: In the following, we discuss these findings by outlining the resulting benefits and challenges for collaborative XAI.
elements.sent-2411-350: Benefits: Explanations support collaborative reasoning, problem-solving, and deliberation.
elements.sent-2411-351: In the best cases, focus groups in our study had an open, communicative, and investigative atmosphere that facilitated the up-taking, sharing, and discussion of information.
elements.sent-2411-352: In these settings, the explanations showed their strengths by allowing for the distribution of reading tasks among group members, providing high levels of detail and breadth if needed, and offering different viewpoints that could be used as argumentative and conversational starting points.
elements.sent-2411-353: In this sense, the explanations fulfilled their aim of supporting collaboration and informed decision-making around deploying a public AI system~\cite{kawakami2024 by creating common ground that participants used as a basis for deliberation.}
elements.sent-2411-354: This deliberation process is the differentiating factor compared to "one-to-one"~\cite{naiseh_explainable_2021 explanation settings, which might allow for more concentrated learning~\cite{bloom_2_1984 , but miss the exchange of knowledge and perspectives that could become central for societal discourse about public AI~\cite{zuger_ai_2023 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-355: While this collaborative atmosphere has several preconditions, such as containing cognitive biases (groupthink~\cite{Janis1972-yo , equality bias~\cite{soton2024 ) and requiring trust between group members~\cite{Johnson1985 , our findings suggest that explanations can be an effective enabler in supporting these crucial group interactions.}}}
elements.sent-2411-356: Regarding learning and understanding, XAI could further benefit from connecting to research showing that small groups can exhibit collective rationality~\cite{moshman_collaborative_1998 , outperform the wisdom of the crowds~\cite{navajas_aggregated_2018 , and solve tasks correctly even if no group member knows the solution beforehand~\cite{smith_why_2009 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-357: Challenges: Explanations must adapt to different groups and require constructive dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-358: While our explanations were effective in supporting interaction and opinion formation in most focus groups, we also observed several challenges resulting from group compositions and trust between participants.
elements.sent-2411-359: While the explanations typically worked well in groups of domain experts, especially G (executive staff) and H (job trainers and counselors), special interaction situations occurred in Groups B and H due to two participants' leading roles in the organizations.

elements.sent-2411-371: In this section, we briefly describe limitations of our study that the reader should consider.
elements.sent-2411-372: Due to the limited sample size, we did not analyze the impact of sex and/or gender on our results, limiting the results' generalizability regarding these aspects but not their overall validity.
elements.sent-2411-373: Further, our participants were recruited from organizations and networks in the same geographical region, perhaps resulting in regional or cultural biases.
elements.sent-2411-374: The presented use case is embedded in a specific sociotechnical context~\cite{ehsan2023 that might affect participants' understanding and perceptions (e.g., perceptions might differ between employability prediction and credit approval), and thus a change in the domain might also change the explanations' effect.}
elements.sent-2411-375: However, this does not limit the transferability of the explanation design, which can be seen as a template that can be adjusted to other use cases.
elements.sent-2411-376: We further note that our participant sample has a bias toward university education in the single interviews, which we addressed by comparing these participants mostly with university-educated participants in the focus groups.
elements.sent-2411-377: In this paper, we proposed and empirically validated a question-driven, modular explanation design for individual and collaborative settings.
elements.sent-2411-378: We conducted an interview study with AI novices in 8 focus groups and 12 single interviews, analyzing the explanations' effect on understanding and decision confidence, participants' perceptions of key information, and the interaction processes in both settings.
elements.sent-2411-379: We found that explanations were effective at supporting and calibrating participants' understanding and at informing individual and collective decision-making.
elements.sent-2411-380: Our findings further showed that our explanations support focused and detail-oriented interaction in individual settings while also being suited to be shared and discussed in group settings.
elements.sent-2411-381: In groups with constructive dynamics, interactions with the explanations led to "collaborative success"~\cite{nokes-malach_when_2015 and informed group discussions about pivotal topics regarding the system's deployment.}
elements.sent-2411-382: For groups in which the explanations were less effective, we suggest adjusting the group dynamic, modifying the explanation’s format, and using co-design approaches to better adapt explanations to various needs.
elements.sent-2411-383: Based on these findings, we present suggestions for the design of explanations for AI novices and outline how their application can support collaborative reasoning and discussion.
elements.sent-2411-384: With this work, we aim to contribute an explanation design approach to the field of collaborative XAI and present implications for how explanations can support stakeholder deliberation about public AI systems.

elements.sent-2411-411: Deploying AI systems in public institutions can have far-reaching consequences for many people, making it a matter of public interest.
elements.sent-2411-412: Providing opportunities for stakeholders to come together, understand these systems, and debate their merits and harms is thus essential.
elements.sent-2411-413: Explainable AI often focuses on individuals, but deliberation benefits from group settings, which are underexplored.
elements.sent-2411-414: To address this gap, we present findings from an interview study with 8 focus groups and 12 individuals.
elements.sent-2411-415: Our findings provide insight into how explanations support AI novices in deliberating alone and in groups.
elements.sent-2411-416: Participants used modular explanations with four information categories to solve tasks and decide about an AI system’s deployment.
elements.sent-2411-417: We found that the explanations supported groups in creating shared understanding and in finding arguments for and against the system's deployment.
elements.sent-2411-418: In comparison, individual participants engaged with explanations in more depth and performed better in the study tasks, but missed an exchange with others.
elements.sent-2411-419: Based on our findings, we provide suggestions on how explanations should be designed to work in group settings and describe their potential use in real-world contexts.
elements.sent-2411-420: With this, our contributions inform XAI research that aims to enable AI novices to understand and deliberate AI systems in the public sector.
elements.sent-2411-421: A growing number of AI systems\footnote{We use the term `AI system' to describe algorithmic systems with machine learning components.}

elements.sent-2411-425: For these reasons, public AI systems should be considered as `matters of public interest'~\cite{zuger_ai_2023 , meaning that they need to be explainable, justifiable, and open for public deliberation~\cite{kawakami2024, soton2024, brown_toward_2019 .}}
elements.sent-2411-426: Explanations can make AI systems more understandable and easier to assess and control~\cite{langer_what_2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-427: Much of explainable AI (XAI) research is focused on individuals, but research has shown that group settings can facilitate the understanding of complex topics~\cite{moshman_collaborative_1998, nokes-malach_when_2015, navajas_aggregated_2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-428: Further, group settings encourage the exchange of views and arguments~\cite{stromer-galley_measuring_2007, smith_why_2009 , which are vital when engaging in deliberation (collectively finding a solution to a problem~\cite{habermas1991structural ).}}

elements.sent-2411-438: Designing explanations that support understanding and deliberation for AI novices in both group and individual settings meets multiple challenges.

elements.sent-2411-452: The following research questions guide the analysis:.
elements.sent-2411-453: Explanations: How does a question-driven, modular explanation design support AI novices’ understanding in groups and individual settings?.
elements.sent-2411-454: Deliberation: How do AI novices use explanations to form opinions and make decisions about AI systems?.
elements.sent-2411-455: Our contributions include i) an explanation design that builds on a question-driven and modular design to accommodate different levels of completeness and soundness and that is suitable for both individual and group settings; ii) an in-depth description of how explanations support participants' understanding and deliberation processes that identifies salient differences between the two settings; iii) an analysis of which type of explanations participants requested most often and perceived as most important; and iv) recommendations regarding the design and use of explanations in group settings.

elements.sent-2411-541: The explanation design comprised 36 question-answer pairs about the \amsalgorithm.
elements.sent-2411-542: Each question belonged to one of four categories, data (format, content, limitations), system details (features, model, examples), usage (operation by and interaction with users), and context (intention of deployment, target group, responsible actors).
elements.sent-2411-543: Each category was further divided into topics with three levels of increasing detail (base level, level 2, level 3).
elements.sent-2411-544: Every explanation was printed on an A5 paper sheet and contained a question (e.g., "Who operates the system?") answered with a brief text or image (cf.. \autoref{fig:explanation_examples ).}

elements.sent-2411-547: The explanation design was intended to allow the users to explore information in a flexible and self-directed manner.
elements.sent-2411-548: To this end, the design used a modular structure, meaning that the explanations were divided into four information categories, which were again subdivided into topics and three levels of detail (base level, level 1, level 2).
elements.sent-2411-549: The four information categories, data, system details, usage, and context were based on research on AI novices' information needs and covered both technical and sociotechnical system aspects~\cite{schmude2024information .}
elements.sent-2411-550: The subdivision of explanation categories into topics and levels of detail organized this broad supply of information while accommodating different needs of information completeness and soundness~\cite{kulesza_too_2013 and introducing a degree of personalization~\cite{chatti_is_2022 .}}
elements.sent-2411-551: The goal of the modular design was thus to create explanations that offered information on every aspect of the AI system, from which participants could select the most relevant according to their information needs and preferences.
elements.sent-2411-552: It further aimed to avoid limitations of ``groupware'' systems~\cite{mandviwalla_1994 by supporting both individual and collaborative interaction, providing multiple user perspectives (e.g., user, decision subject), and synchronizing interaction with the material.}

elements.sent-2411-575: This section provides descriptions and motivation for the study elements: the introduction of the use case, the explanation phase comprising orientation, tasks, and the group decision, and participants' individual reports.
elements.sent-2411-576: Participants received initial information about the \amsalgorithm in the form of a mock newspaper article inspired by an Austrian newspaper publication from 2019~\cite{standard_ams_2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-577: The article provided key information about the system's basic workings, goals, and deployment context and featured the opinions of employers and employee associations about its merits and risks.
elements.sent-2411-578: The presentation format was chosen to provide an introductory summary of the AI system using a familiar layout and non-technical language while highlighting both the pros and cons of the system's deployment.
elements.sent-2411-579: Thus, The article served as a basic introduction to the use case, which aimed to approximate the amount of information participants might receive through the media.
elements.sent-2411-580: This way, participants received a baseline of information with which to assess their initial understanding, deployment decision, and decision confidence.
elements.sent-2411-581: Further, this introduction served to outline relevant aspects that could be explored using the explanations.
elements.subsubsection-2411-016: Explanation phase (orientation, tasks, and group decision).
elements.sent-2411-582: For orientation, participants received the explanation overview (\autoref{fig:explanation_overview ) and base-level explanations (the first row of explanations in each category) and had 15 minutes (focus groups) or 20 minutes (single interviews) to become familiar with the structure and explore topics of interest.}
elements.sent-2411-583: Explanations were provided as single A5 sheets to promote the physical sharing and exchanging of explanations.
elements.sent-2411-584: Participants could freely decide which explanations to request and read, and whether to share and discuss information with others.
elements.sent-2411-585: For the tasks, participants received the case of Mr. Harald G.\footnote{The case example was inspired by~\citet{allhutter_bericht_ams-algorithmus_2020 and adapted to this study, as depicted in the appendix.}}
elements.sent-2411-586: Participants had 15 minutes (focus groups) or 20 minutes (single interviews) to solve four tasks pertaining to this case.
elements.sent-2411-587: All questions could be answered with information from explanations in different categories and levels of detail.
elements.sent-2411-588: Whereas tasks 1, 3, and 4 required locating information, task 2 could be solved in two ways (aside from guessing): by either giving an estimate based on the rough weightings in the system details base explanations or calculating the precise employment score.
elements.sent-2411-589: Participants could access and request all explanations and discuss possible solutions.
elements.sent-2411-590: These were the four tasks (correct answers underlined):.
elements.sent-2411-591: Task 1: Can Harald change the data stored about him (e.g., to correct it)? (yes / no) Task 2: In which group of employment chance does the system categorize Harald? (high (>66\%) / medium (<66\% \& >25\%) / low (<25\%)) Task 3: Which support measures will Harald receive? (qualifying measures, such as courses and training / stabilization and increased supervision / none) Task 4: Can Harald appeal against this decision? (yes / no).
elements.sent-2411-592: For the group decision, focus group participants had 10 minutes to discuss the system's deployment and were asked to collectively decide whether to accept or reject it.
elements.sent-2411-593: This was meant to simulate a small referendum in which each participant's voice counted for the final outcome.
elements.sent-2411-594: If no consensual decision was reached in time, participants were asked how the situation should be resolved (e.g., majority vote).
elements.sent-2411-595: They were further made aware of the option to state conditions for the system's deployment.
elements.sent-2411-596: Participants were asked for individual reports before and after the explanation phase.
elements.sent-2411-597: At both points, participants reported understanding (5-point scale), deployment decision (yes / no), and decision confidence (5-point scale) to examine the effect of the explanation phase.
elements.sent-2411-598: In report II, participants also reported the explanation categories that were most helpful for their understanding (multiple choice) and most influential to their decisions (multiple choice); focus group participants additionally reported perceived inclusion in the group and the discussions' influence on their decision (5-point scales).
elements.sent-2411-599: During report II, the investigator asked participants interview questions about their interaction with the explanations, understanding processes, prioritized information, and situational aspects.

elements.sent-2411-603: Participants took the corresponding paper slip for each report, wrote their answer, and threw it in a gathering container that hid it from view.

elements.subsection-2411-024: RQ1-Explanations: How does a question-driven, modular explanation design support AI novices’ understanding in groups and individual settings?
elements.sent-2411-645: To examine how AI novices used the explanations to understand the study's use case, we analyzed their self-reports, articulations, and interactions in both settings.
elements.sent-2411-646: We found that each setting supported different aspects of understanding, suggesting a trade-off.
elements.sent-2411-647: We first describe how the explanations contributed to shared understanding and `collaborative success' in groups (\ref{sec:results_groups_benefits ) and continue with the explanations' role in instances of `collaborative failure'~\cite{nokes-malach_when_2015 (\ref{sec:results_groups_drawbacks ), summarized in~\autoref{fig:findings_group_understanding .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-648: We then describe individuals' interactions with the explanations (\ref{sec:results_individuals ), participants' feedback on the explanation design (\ref{sec:results_explanation_design ), and summarize the benefits and drawbacks of both settings for XAI (\ref{sec:rq1_summary ).}}}
elements.subsubsection-2411-024: Groups' benefits: Shared understanding and increased engagement.
elements.sent-2411-649: In the best cases, groups leveraged the modular explanation structure to use distributed cognition~\cite{keil_folkscience_2003 , meaning that participants processed information in parallel and then combined it.}
elements.sent-2411-650: We use the term shared understanding to capture interactions that realized distributed cognition.
elements.sent-2411-651: Examples of such interactions included locating information together, sharing information with others, discussing interpretations, debating task solutions, and querying and explaining (a question by a group participant invites other participants to contribute).
elements.sent-2411-652: The explanations only afforded this set of interactions to groups, as they required social interaction with other participants.
elements.sent-2411-653: For example, in Group C, participant C1 read the first study task aloud and asked for input (querying), after which the group discussed solutions (explaining):.
elements.sent-2411-654: Duty of care -- he could get married or have children.
elements.sent-2411-655: He could change his service sector.
elements.sent-2411-656: He could change his career.. Impairment...
elements.sent-2411-657: He doesn't have to specify the knee problem.
elements.sent-2411-658: Interactions such as querying and explaining and discussing interpretations rely on collaboration between participants to `share working memory resources', `complement others' knowledge', `re-expose information', and `correct errors'.
elements.sent-2411-659: These aspects of collaboration are described as cognitive mechanisms of `collaborative success'~\cite{nokes-malach_when_2015 and provide groups with multiple ways to tackle explanations.}
elements.sent-2411-660: For example, participants tended to work through information about usage and context alone or in pairs but raised explanations with the group when they were difficult or piqued their interest.
elements.sent-2411-661: This was often the case with explanations about system details, which included the most numerical information but also were an important key to understanding the system's calculations.
elements.sent-2411-662: We describe the process of using other's understanding to close gaps in one's own as outsourcing~\cite{keil2006 .}
elements.sent-2411-663: As understanding AI systems involves interacting with a variety of different information categories (e.g., technical, political, social), outsourcing provides a way to hand information to the team member most competent in this category.
elements.sent-2411-664: For example, in Group B, B2 expressed their appreciation for B3's help in solving study task 2: ``It was a math problem.
elements.sent-2411-667: With your help, we were able to recognize these weak points.'' In contrast to querying and explaining, which participants used to invite input or spark conversation, outsourcing was thus used for the active delegation of an impeded understanding process.
elements.sent-2411-668: Whether groups used this collaboration depended on participants' relationships and the group's social dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-669: The explanations also served to support these `social mechanisms' of collaboration, by drawing the group's attention to certain aspects of the AI system and encouraging them to share their experiences and opinions.
elements.sent-2411-670: We present an excerpt from Group G as an illustration.
elements.sent-2411-671: Here, participant G4 shared an explanation that documented the algorithm’s impact on two job-seekers (`joint management of attention'), which prompted G2 and G3 to discuss interpretations (`increased engagement').
elements.sent-2411-672: This interaction established `common ground' that the group later used for deliberation:.
elements.sent-2411-673: Look, ``What chances would the system calculate for Martin and Schifteh?'' \colorbox{GroupBlue {G2 Schifteh is probably worse off, isn't she? \colorbox{GroupYellow {G4 Schifteh has a 30\% chance of employment and Martin 52\%, even though Schifteh has a degree and would be working in the IT sector.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-674: A later excerpt in Section~\ref{sec:results_delib_disagreement further shows that explanations also led participants to `negotiate multiple perspectives'.}
elements.sent-2411-675: Addressing both sides of collaboration, cognitive and social, here is an important goal when supporting group understanding.
elements.sent-2411-676: It can be assumed that when explanations succeed in doing both, they can provide participants with more comprehensive and more complex information than individual settings.
elements.sent-2411-677: In line with this, previous work in HCI has found that group interaction boosts task performance compared to individual settings~\cite{karadzhov_delidata_2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-678: However, in our study, individuals surprisingly performed better in the study tasks than groups, as discussed in Section~\ref{sec:results_individuals .}
elements.sent-2411-679: Even so, we argue that the set of interactions (shared understanding) enabled through the combination of our explanation design and the group setting presents important pathways to help AI novices understand algorithmic systems.
elements.sent-2411-680: These interactions can be especially useful when group members have different domain expertise and information needs, as they can use complementary knowledge, memory, and perspectives to make sense of information.
elements.sent-2411-681: At the same time, as noted earlier, these interactions are partly dependent on the group dynamic.
elements.sent-2411-682: Positive interactions like those described were especially frequent in Groups G and H (job counselors and trainers), where participants knew and trusted each other.
elements.sent-2411-683: In contrast, the next subsection describes instances where groups encountered challenges in understanding, illustrating the importance of social mechanisms.
elements.subsubsection-2411-025: Groups' drawbacks: Process loss and susceptibility to social dynamics
elements.sent-2411-684: In some of the focus groups, participants lost track of information, forgot their train of thought, or abandoned understanding altogether.
elements.sent-2411-685: We summarize these effects under the term impeded understanding and its final result as abandoned understanding.
elements.sent-2411-686: We found that impeded understanding occurred due to explanation design flaws and co-occurred with adverse social dynamics, resulting in `process loss' (groups falling short of their potential performance~\cite{kerr_group_2004 ).}
elements.sent-2411-687: For some participants, the benefits of the explanations' modular structure turned into disadvantages when it hampered them in navigating and retrieving information.
elements.sent-2411-688: Such impeded interactions included cumbersome information uptake, being overwhelmed by information, and relying on intuition over information.
elements.sent-2411-689: Further, for some participants, the group setting contributed to these impediments.
elements.sent-2411-690: For example, participant B3 stated that ``For me, it doesn't make sense to [...] split up [the explanations], and everyone reads a part, that's actually not enough.'' As before, these impediments can be connected to cognitive mechanisms of `collaborative failure'~\cite{nokes-malach_when_2015 .}
elements.sent-2411-691: When groups had difficulties in interacting with the explanations, they also incurred `memory coordination cost' (increased cognitive load) and `retrieval strategy disruption' (losing train of thought).
elements.sent-2411-692: We illustrate these mechanisms with an exchange from Group G.
elements.sent-2411-693: Although this group was composed of participants with university education, it did not succeed in calculating the employment chance for study task 2, in contrast to single interview participants with the same education.
elements.sent-2411-694: Where is the piece of paper with this terrible matrix? [...] \colorbox{GroupBlue {G2 I still don't understand which value to put.}}
elements.sent-2411-695: To calculate it, I need an exact value for the weighting.
elements.sent-2411-697: I believe that he [Harald] has over 25\%.
elements.sent-2411-698: And she also has 2 minuses [...] and a plus.
